raw_case:
Page kidney in a child with ureteropelvic junction obstruction of lower moiety in a partial duplex system
Reports of hydronephrosis-induced hypertension in pediatric patients are rare. A 7-year-old girl with hypertension was referred to our hospital with left hydronephrosis caused by ureteropelvic junction obstruction of the lower moiety in a partial duplex system. Because the lower moiety was almost nonfunctional, a laparoscopic heminephrectomy was performed. Antihypertensive drugs were unnecessary on the day after surgery. This is the first report of a Page kidney in a patient with ureteropelvic junction obstruction of the lower moiety. The cause of hypertension was evaluated based on the plasma renin activity and pathological findings of the lower moiety.

Highlights

•The patient with hydronephrosis was successfully diagnosed with Page kidney.•This is the first report of Page kidney evaluated by immunostaining.•Pediatric patients with hypertension and severe hydronephrosis may have Page kidney.1

Introduction

Page kidney (PK) is a rare condition that presents with hypertension (HT) secondary to compression of the renal parenchyma.1,2 Most cases of PK have been reported in adults following trauma,2 and nontraumatic PK in children is extremely rare.3 Moreover, PK typically arises from external compression of the renal parenchyma.1,2 However, we propose that both external compression can cause PK but also internal-to-external compression of the renal parenchyma due to severe hydronephrosis.Herein, we report a case of PK in a child with ureteropelvic junction obstruction (UPJO) of the lower moiety in a partial duplex system that was diagnosed based on postoperative normalization of blood pressure and plasma renin activity (PRA) after laparoscopic heminephrectomy (LHN) for hydronephrosis of the lower moiety.2

Case report

A 7-year-old girl with abdominal distension was admitted to a pediatric hospital. The patient had a history of immunoglobulin A vasculitis. She was diagnosed with HT and left hydronephrosis and was started on antihypertensive medication. Subsequently, the patient was referred to our hospital for further investigation of left hydronephrosis.Abdominal computed tomography revealed a left partial duplex system with UPJO of the left lower moiety (Fig. 1A and B). Differential renal function (DRF) assessed using a dimercaptosuccinic acid scan was 31.1 % for the left kidney and 1.7 % for the left lower moiety (Fig. 1C). Given the significantly reduced function of the left lower moiety, we planned for LHN.Initially, cystoscopy was performed under general anesthesia with the patient in the lithotomy position. Cystoscopy revealed that the right and left ureteral orifices were in normal anatomical positions. Subsequent retrograde pyelography revealed a left-sided partial duplex system. A 4-F ureteral catheter was placed in the ureter of the left upper moiety. The patient was then placed in an oblique position, with the operated side elevated. LHN was performed using the retrocolic approach. A 12-mm trocar was inserted as a camera port into the peritoneal cavity through an incision in the upper part of the umbilicus, and two 5-mm trocars were inserted as working ports. The procedure revealed a dilated pelvis in the lower moiety that significantly obstructed the operative field. Subsequently, a controlled incision was made into the dilated pelvis to aspirate renal urine, facilitating adequate visualization for further surgery (Fig. 2A). Laparoscopic exploration successfully identified the Y-shaped left ureter. Arteries and veins supplying the lower moiety were clearly visualized. Using LigaSure bipolar forceps (Medtronic PLC; Minneapolis, MN, USA), the vessels were meticulously sealed and transected (Fig. 2B and C). Similarly, the ureter of the lower moiety was transected and closed using LigaSure (Fig. 2D). Subsequently, the lower moiety was dissected using a monopolar hook and removed through the umbilicus (Fig. 3A).Antihypertensive drugs were discontinued because the patient's blood pressure normalized on the day after surgery. The levels of PRA and aldosterone after surgery also normalized to 2.8 ng/mL/hr and 93 pg/mL. However, the preoperative levels of PRA and aldosterone were high at 8.5 ng/mL/hr and 254 pg/mL, respectively. Focal glomerular structures were observed in the renal parenchyma of the resected tissues (Fig. 3B). Immunostaining with an anti-renin antibody (ab212197; Abcam, Cambridge, England) showed that renin was intensely and focally expressed in the juxtaglomerular (JG) apparatus (Fig. 3C and D). Based on the normalization of blood pressure and PRA after LHN, the patient was diagnosed with HT due to PK disease. Ten years have passed since the operation, the DRF of the left upper moiety has been preserved, and the patient's blood pressure has remained normal.3

Discussion

The pediatric nephrological literature identifies UPJO as a cause of HT.4 However, HT caused by UPJO is rare,5,6 although patients with UPJO may develop HT later.7 Although the pathogenesis of HT in patients with UPJO is unclear, hyperreninemia may be a cause thereof.8 Moreover, it has been reported that hypertrophy of the JG apparatus is observed in the renal parenchyma,8,9 and renin is strongly expressed in the JG apparatus by pathological examination of patients with UPJO complicated by HT.9PK is a cause of HT and is typically associated with trauma in adult patients.1,2 PK is diagnosed by imaging findings that show that subcapsular contents, such as hemorrhage or urine, compress the renal parenchyma.2 Recently, rare nontraumatic cases in pediatric patients have been reported.3 Although the pathogenesis of PK is also unclear, one of the causes is thought to be the activation of the renin–angiotensin system due to ischemia of the renal parenchyma by the compression of the renal parenchyma.10 In addition to imaging findings, there are also reports of PK showing an increase in PRA.11 Furthermore, an adult case of PK due to intraperitoneal compression of the kidney has been reported.12 This suggests that PK can also develop by compression of the renal parenchyma from contents other than subcapsular.The patient was diagnosed with HT because of PK for the following reasons: First, the blood pressure of the patient normalized immediately after LHN, although preoperative imaging findings showed that the severely dilated renal pelvis compressed the renal parenchyma of the lower moiety. Second, the PRA level normalized after LHN, although the level of PRA was high before LHN. Therefore, we concluded that HT was due to hyperreninemia caused by the compression of the renal parenchyma. This is the first report of PK demonstrating high renin expression in the JG apparatus by pathological evaluation.PK is usually diagnosed as a result of trauma or some events.1, 2, 3,10, 11, 12 In other words, patients with PK seem to be in a situation where it is easy to check the presence or absence of HT by checking their vital signs. However, the patient in this case had not been diagnosed with HT until the patient was admitted to the referring hospital because the patient solely presented with abdominal distension. Therefore, the timing of HT onset in this patient was unclear. However, the pathogenesis of HT in some patients with UPJO may be similar to that of PK if HT in both patients with PK and patients with UPJO is caused by hyperreninemia.8, 9, 10, 114

Conclusion

We successfully diagnosed PK based on the normalization of blood pressure and PRA after LHN. The possibility of PK should be considered when HT complicates severe hydronephrosis because HT is extremely rare in pediatric patients. Prompt surgical intervention for hydronephrosis complicated by HT may protect renal function.

CRediT authorship contribution statement

Hidenori Nishio: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Conceptualization. Kentaro Mizuno: Writing – review & editing. Takuya Sakata: Data curation. Daisuke Matsumoto: Data curation. Hideyuki Kamisawa: Writing – review & editing. Satoshi Kurokawa: Writing – review & editing. Akihiro Nakane: Writing – review & editing. Tetsuji Maruyama: Supervision. Takahiro Yasui: Supervision. Yutaro Hayashi: Supervision.

Financial disclosure

None.

Declaration of competing interest

None.

generate_case.case_summary:
- Patient Information: 7-year-old female
- Chief Complaint: Abdominal distension
- History of Present Illness: Hypertension and left hydronephrosis caused by ureteropelvic junction obstruction (UPJO) of the lower moiety in a partial duplex system. The lower moiety had significantly reduced function.
- Past Medical History: History of immunoglobulin A vasculitis.
- Personal History: None.
- Family History: None.
- Physical Examination: None specifically noted in the report.
- Ancillary Tests:
  - Abdominal CT: Left partial duplex system with UPJO of the lower moiety.
  - Dimercaptosuccinic acid scan: Differential renal function of 31.1% for the left kidney and 1.7% for the left lower moiety.
  - Preoperative renin-angiotensin system labs: Elevated plasma renin activity (8.5 ng/mL/hr) and aldosterone (254 pg/mL).
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Consideration of Etiologies for Hypertension (HT):**
   - The patient's age and presentation (severe HT and hydronephrosis) directed focus on secondary causes of HT, including renal/renovascular disorders.

2. **Analysis of Left Hydronephrosis and Its Impact:**
   - Imaging revealed a dilated lower moiety compressing renal parenchyma in the partial duplex system. UPJO with loss of function of the lower moiety (<2% function) was consistent with obstruction leading to severe hydronephrosis.

3. **Investigation of Pathogenesis for HT:**
   - Preoperative elevated PRA and pathological findings (juxtaglomerular renin expression) indicated renin-driven HT, consistent with a Page kidney (PK) mechanism—renal parenchyma compression leading to ischemia and renin overproduction.

4. **Exclusion of Other Causes:**
   - No evidence of systemic or endocrine causes complicating HT.
   - Imaging and surgical findings confirmed local obstruction and parenchymal compression as primary contributors.

5. **Confirmation by Surgical Intervention:**
   - Normalized blood pressure and PRA levels immediately post-laparoscopic heminephrectomy (LHN) confirmed HT as secondary to PK due to hydronephrosis-induced parenchymal compression.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Page kidney (PK) secondary to ureteropelvic junction obstruction (UPJO) of the lower moiety in a partial duplex system, causing severe hydronephrosis and renin-driven hypertension.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Page kidney due to ureteropelvic junction obstruction.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's start by looking at the patient's information. She's a 7-year-old girl with abdominal distension as the chief complaint. Her history includes hypertension and left hydronephrosis due to UPJO in a partial duplex system affecting the lower moiety, which has reduced function.

First, I need to connect her symptoms with her medical history. Abdominal distension can have various causes, like gastrointestinal issues, fluid accumulation, or organ enlargement. But given her history of UPJO and hydronephrosis, maybe the distension is related to her kidney issues. 

Hydronephrosis from UPJO can cause the kidney to swell, which might lead to a palpable mass or distension. The left lower moiety's function is only 1.7%, which is really low. That suggests that part of the kidney isn't working well, possibly leading to enlargement from obstruction.

She also has a history of IgA vasculitis. IgA vasculitis can affect the kidneys, causing nephritis. But in this case, the main issue seems structural with the UPJO. However, maybe there's some overlap or residual effects contributing to her current state.

The lab results show elevated renin and aldosterone. High renin and aldosterone usually point towards secondary hypertension, possibly due to renal artery stenosis or reduced blood flow to the kidneys. In her case, the UPJO causing hydronephrosis might be leading to renal ischemia in the affected kidney moiety, triggering the renin-angiotensin system. This could explain her hypertension as well.

The duplex kidney system means there are two separate pelvicalyceal systems on the left side. The lower moiety's obstruction (UPJO) would cause urine to back up, leading to hydronephrosis. The diminished function (1.7%) indicates significant damage or atrophy in that part. The overall left kidney function is 31.1%, which is lower than normal (normally around 45-55% for each kidney). So the left kidney's overall function is compromised, mostly due to the lower moiety issue.

Abdominal distension in a child with these findings might be due to a massively dilated renal pelvis or ureter from the obstruction. The distension could also be from ascites, but with normal other histories, that's less likely. Alternatively, a large kidney due to hydronephrosis could present as abdominal swelling.

Putting this all together, the primary issue is the UPJO in the lower moiety of a duplex system leading to significant hydronephrosis and reduced function. The elevated renin and aldosterone support that the hypertension is secondary to the renal issue. The abdominal distension is likely from the enlarged kidney due to the obstruction. IgA vasculitis history might have contributed to kidney issues in the past, but current findings are more structural.

Therefore, the final diagnosis would be related to the UPJO in the duplex system causing these complications. The hypertension is a consequence of the renal pathology here.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient is a 7-year-old female with abdominal distension as the chief complaint. Her history includes hypertension and left hydronephrosis secondary to ureteropelvic junction obstruction (UPJO) of the lower moiety in a partial duplex system, which has severely reduced function (1.7% on DMSA scan). This structural anomaly likely contributes to both her hypertension and abdominal distension.

<step 2> Elevated plasma renin activity and aldosterone levels suggest activation of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS), typically secondary to reduced renal perfusion. This aligns with the obstructed lower moiety causing renal ischemia, which drives hypertension through RAAS activation.

<step 3> The abdominal distension is likely due to the grossly dilated renal pelvis (hydronephrosis) from UPJO in the lower moiety of the duplex system. The duplex system’s anatomical complexity exacerbates obstruction-related swelling, contributing to visible abdominal enlargement.

<step 4> The history of IgA vasculitis, while notable, does not directly explain the current presentation. The primary pathophysiology here is structural (UPJO in a duplex system) rather than immune-mediated, though prior renal damage from vasculitis may have compounded functional impairment.

<step 5> The combination of anatomical obstruction (UPJO), reduced renal function in the lower moiety, RAAS-driven hypertension, and hydronephrosis-induced abdominal distension confirms a unified diagnosis centered on the congenital duplex system anomaly.

### Answer:
Left lower moiety ureteropelvic junction obstruction (UPJO) in a partial duplex kidney system with secondary hypertension and hydronephrosis-induced abdominal distension.

reasoning_eval.step: The patient is a 7-year-old female with abdominal distension as the chief complaint. Her history includes hypertension and left hydronephrosis secondary to ureteropelvic junction obstruction (UPJO) of the lower moiety in a partial duplex system, which has severely reduced function (1.7% on DMSA scan). This structural anomaly likely contributes to both her hypertension and abdominal distension.

Elevated plasma renin activity and aldosterone levels suggest activation of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS), typically secondary to reduced renal perfusion. This aligns with the obstructed lower moiety causing renal ischemia, which drives hypertension through RAAS activation.

The abdominal distension is likely due to the grossly dilated renal pelvis (hydronephrosis) from UPJO in the lower moiety of the duplex system. The duplex system’s anatomical complexity exacerbates obstruction-related swelling, contributing to visible abdominal enlargement.

The history of IgA vasculitis, while notable, does not directly explain the current presentation. The primary pathophysiology here is structural (UPJO in a duplex system) rather than immune-mediated, though prior renal damage from vasculitis may have compounded functional impairment.

The combination of anatomical obstruction (UPJO), reduced renal function in the lower moiety, RAAS-driven hypertension, and hydronephrosis-induced abdominal distension confirms a unified diagnosis centered on the congenital duplex system anomaly.

Step 2: Imaging revealed a dilated lower moiety compressing renal parenchyma in the partial duplex system, consistent with UPJO with loss of function of the lower moiety (<2% function), leading to severe hydronephrosis.

Step 3: Preoperative elevated PRA and juxtaglomerular renin expression indicated renin-driven hypertension, consistent with a Page kidney (PK) mechanism—renal parenchyma compression leading to ischemia and renin overproduction.
Step 4: No evidence of systemic or endocrine causes complicating hypertension; imaging and surgical findings confirmed local obstruction and parenchymal compression as primary contributors.
Step 5: Post-laparoscopic heminephrectomy (LHN), normalized blood pressure and PRA levels confirmed hypertension as secondary to PK due to hydronephrosis-induced parenchymal compression.
Final diagnosis: Page kidney (PK) secondary to ureteropelvic junction obstruction (UPJO) of the lower moiety in a partial duplex system, causing severe hydronephrosis and renin-driven hypertension.
